jokes.text: Animal sound effects (chimpanzee shrieking, pigs grunting, jackals cackling) overlaid on the subway car crammed with hot, sweaty strangers
jokes.setup: The women squeeze through a packed, sweltering NYC subway car in a heat wave
jokes.punchline: The show literalizes the 'animals in a cage' misery of summer subway commuting with actual wildlife sound effects, culminating in Abbi's deadpan 'Ugh, typical.'
jokes.explanation: Earns its 8+ by executing a purely cinematic joke with real economy — no explanation, no wink, just the sounds and Abbi's resigned 'typical.' The concept is genuinely original (not just 'it's crowded,' but a full sonic transformation of the car into a zoo) and the deadpan delivery caps it perfectly. It also functions as efficient scene-setting for the episode's central theme of NYC summer as primal hell.

jokes.text: Ilana is clearly doing something disgusting/sexual on the subway — moaning, prompting Abbi's exasperated 'Ilana, come on.'
jokes.setup: Amid the chaos of the packed subway, Abbi is trying to navigate through
jokes.punchline: Ilana is audibly moaning — the joke is entirely in what we can't see but can clearly infer, with Abbi's weary tone suggesting this is not the first time
jokes.explanation: A solid character beat — the joke lives in what isn't said. Abbi's 'come on' implies total normalcy-of-abnormality in their friendship. The visual is funnier than any line could be, but without seeing it the transcript version is a partial joke.

jokes.text: Whatever is discovered at the back of the subway car causes Abbi to gag and both women to flee in horror
jokes.setup: They were told they needed to go to the back of the train
jokes.punchline: The back of the train apparently contains something so horrifying it causes genuine gagging — the mystery of what it is, left entirely to imagination, is the joke
jokes.explanation: A pure visual gag that only works on screen. The transcript gives us reactions ('Oh, my God,' 'Gagging') but not the content. Presumably a strong visual joke given the extended reaction sequence, but in transcript form it's a partial read.

jokes.text: Ilana's theory that fat men's dicks get 'sucked back inside their bodies' delivered with total scientific confidence
jokes.setup: Casual walk-and-talk conversation between the two friends
jokes.punchline: 'It's not like it's science suction, Bill Nye!' — Ilana invokes a celebrity scientist to defend pseudoscientific genital theory
jokes.explanation: High character integration — only Ilana would deliver body-fat-to-penis-size pseudoscience with evangelical certainty, then name-drop Bill Nye to dismiss Abbi's skepticism. The 'science suction' phrasing is wonderfully specific gibberish. Scores 8+ because it layers absurdist logic, character voice, and a genuinely funny specific celebrity invocation all in under ten words.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'What, so it's like the best optical illusion of all time?' / Ilana: 'Exactly. Like, um, Mount Rushmore, how it looks, you know, like the presidents?'
jokes.setup: Abbi offers a reasonable analogy (optical illusion) to Ilana's penis theory
jokes.punchline: Ilana enthusiastically agrees, then gives a 'example' of an optical illusion that is just... a sculpture accurately depicting what it depicts. Mount Rushmore 'looks like the presidents' because it IS the presidents — it's not an illusion at all.
jokes.explanation: This earns its 8+ because the joke operates on multiple levels simultaneously: (1) Ilana confidently uses a non-example as an example, (2) Abbi's correction ('it was carved by an artist') is a perfect straight-man beat, and (3) it reveals Ilana doesn't know what an optical illusion is, which is funnier than if she'd just said something offensive. The Mount Rushmore specificity is what elevates it — a random wrong example would be weaker. This is distinctly Ilana-brained logic.

jokes.text: Abbi's correction: 'Well, Mount Rushmore is actually a sculpture. Like, it was carved by an artist.'
jokes.setup: Ilana uses Mount Rushmore as an example of an optical illusion
jokes.punchline: Abbi's utterly calm, patient, almost professorial correction of a basic factual error is funnier than any outraged reaction would be
jokes.explanation: Works as a standalone deadpan beat — the 'Like, it was carved by an artist' elaboration is the extra twist of the knife, as if Ilana might not have grasped 'sculpture.' Strong character work for Abbi as the perpetual, beleaguered corrector.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Dude, this heat is like killing me right now... All my senses are heightened, even my ethnic ambiguity. I was catcalled today in what I believe is creole.'
jokes.setup: Ilana complains about the heat
jokes.punchline: She pivots to saying the heat heightens 'even my ethnic ambiguity,' then proves it with the Creole catcall anecdote — treating ethnic ambiguity as a physical sense that can be sharpened
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest lines. 'Even my ethnic ambiguity' treats ambiguous racial identity as a quantifiable sensory capacity — a genuinely original formulation. The Creole catcall as proof-of-concept is perfectly specific and absurd. High character integration: this is quintessential Ilana reframing a mundane indignity (street harassment) as a personal superpower. The 'even' implies there's a hierarchy of heightened senses she's running through, which implies a whole inner world we don't see.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Well, I'm on the edge of like swamp ass right now. I need some A/C.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been waxing poetic about loving the heat and having heightened senses
jokes.punchline: Abbi's response cuts through all of Ilana's romanticism with blunt, unglamorous physical reality: swamp ass
jokes.explanation: A solid character contrast beat — Ilana is transcendent about the heat, Abbi is suffering physically. The specificity of 'swamp ass' (vs. just 'I'm sweating') is what makes it land. Also plants the A/C need that drives the whole episode's plot.

jokes.text: Ilana and Abbi arrive at a drugstore/pharmacy and Ilana announces 'We're gonna have to throw these out' while apparently looking at their underwear/clothes in the store mirror under good lighting
jokes.setup: They've been walking in extreme heat and have just entered a store
jokes.punchline: The 'incredible' drugstore lighting reveals the full extent of their sweat damage — the joke is the specificity of using retail lighting as a diagnostic tool for heat-related clothing destruction
jokes.explanation: A functional scene-setting joke that continues the swamp-ass/heat theme. The 'incredible lighting' line suggests self-examination in unflattering fluorescent light, a relatable bit. Competent but not special.

jokes.text: Ilana's Colin Farrell sex tape monologue: timestamping specific moments ('8:58 he is feasting on her [bleep] like it is a dang milk shake'), complete with Irish accent impersonation
jokes.setup: Abbi mentions male Stacey is cooking her dinner; Ilana recommends she watch the Colin Farrell sex tape 'to get back in the swing of things'
jokes.punchline: Ilana has the tape memorized down to the second, performs it in an Irish accent, and provides a timestamped breakdown across three separate moments as if giving a film recommendation
jokes.explanation: Earns its 8+ through escalation structure and character integration. The joke has three distinct escalating beats: (1) the recommendation itself, (2) the specific timestamp with the milkshake simile, (3) Ilana doing a bit-perfect Irish accent as Farrell. The milkshake metaphor is a perfect piece of Ilana-speak — enthusiastic, visual, weirdly reverent. Then the Irish accent is a genuine performance commitment that elevates it. The structured timestamp breakdown ('8:58... 9:44... 12:47') turns a raunchy recommendation into a guided tour, which is a genuinely original framing device.

jokes.text: Abbi responding in an Irish accent: ''Twas 8:58 you said, sire?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is doing a Colin Farrell impression mid-sex-tape-breakdown
jokes.punchline: Abbi spontaneously joins the bit in a terrible Irish accent, turning a recommendation into a two-person dramatic reading of a celebrity sex tape
jokes.explanation: The 'sire' is the perfect touch — Abbi's accent has slipped into something vaguely medieval, which is funnier than if she'd done it well. It also shows the organic improv energy of their friendship: Abbi can't help but join a bit even when she shouldn't. 'Sire' also implies she's cast Ilana as royalty in this imagined scenario, which is a whole extra layer.

jokes.text: The Colin Farrell breakdown is interrupted when they realize a New York mom with a stroller is within earshot — 'Oh. So sorry. We love New York moms.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is mid-sex-tape-timestamp breakdown in a public pharmacy
jokes.punchline: The sudden awareness of audience, followed by 'We love New York moms' as if that fixes it, is a great cringe beat
jokes.explanation: 'We love New York moms' is the kind of non-apology that makes things worse — it addresses the presence but not the offense, which is perfectly on-brand for both characters. Solid cringe-comedy structure.

jokes.text: Abbi buys the underwear she's been wearing in the store because they are 'now, like, sort of part of my body'
jokes.setup: The store visit started as a cooling-off stop; Abbi has apparently been trying on or holding underwear
jokes.punchline: The swamp ass situation has become so severe that clothing has physiologically bonded to her — presented with total calm
jokes.explanation: A good callback to the swamp ass setup with a funny specific escalation — she has to BUY them now. 'Sort of part of my body' is a nicely gross formulation. The deadpan acceptance is very Abbi.

jokes.text: Male Stacey reveals his 'plan': invite Abbi over, hide that he has no A/C, get her clothes off via heat
jokes.setup: 'My plan is working. Get you over here, hide the A/C, get those clothes off.'
jokes.punchline: 'You don't actually have an air conditioner, do you?' / 'No, I don't.'
jokes.explanation: Clean setup/punchline structure — the 'plan' monologue sets up the reveal that there's no A/C. The concept of a heat-wave seduction plan that involves hiding the absence of cooling is solidly absurd. The direct, unembarrassed confession makes it land.

jokes.text: Abbi alone in the bathroom talking herself down — 'Just cool down, Abbi. You're cool, you're cool... Cool mode.' — then using a hair dryer to blow-dry her sweat
jokes.setup: Abbi has excused herself from the date to handle her swamp ass
jokes.punchline: Her internal pep talk escalates to the invented concept of 'Cool mode,' then she deploys a hair dryer on her own body — the opposite of the correct tool for the job
jokes.explanation: The self-affirmation sequence ('You're cool, you're cool... Cool mode') is funny as a verbal bit — the increasingly absurd reassurance building to the invented phrase 'Cool mode.' Then the hair dryer escalates it to physical comedy: she's using a heat-generating device to cool herself down, which is both wrong and exactly the kind of logic Abbi would use under pressure. Strong character integration: Abbi's anxious self-management is a core trait.

jokes.text: Ilana at a party declaring 'All Hollywood media is porn, and all porn is kiddie porn' to a stranger
jokes.setup: Ilana is in conversation with a stranger at a party who mentions their daughter loves Frozen
jokes.punchline: Ilana agrees enthusiastically about Frozen, then pivots to a maximalist media theory that collapses all of culture into child exploitation — delivered as casual party chat
jokes.explanation: A high character-integration joke — only Ilana would connect Frozen to a grand unified theory of media as child pornography in one sentence at a party. The syllogistic structure ('all X is Y, all Y is Z, therefore all X is Z') gives it a false intellectual rigor that's part of the joke. The stranger's reaction (a delighted 'Ooh') is the secondary punchline — someone is enjoying this.

jokes.text: The stranger excuses herself to 'run to the little girl's room' and Ilana immediately calls this 'rape culture language'
jokes.setup: The stranger uses the common euphemism 'little girl's room'
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately applies her media-theory framework to the most mundane possible linguistic target, treating a bathroom euphemism as evidence of systemic oppression
jokes.explanation: A strong callback that escalates by applying Ilana's grand theory to an absurdly small target. The joke works because Ilana's framework is internally consistent — she's not wrong that 'little girl's room' is infantilizing language — but applying it instantaneously to a stranger leaving for the bathroom is a perfect illustration of how her worldview operates at all scales simultaneously. 'Wow. Wow, wow, wow, oh.' from the stranger is a great punchline beat.

jokes.text: Ilana corrects someone for saying 'little girl's room': 'That is rape culture language right there.'
jokes.setup: Someone (possibly a dad) says 'I'm gonna run to the little girl's room' (a common bathroom euphemism)
jokes.punchline: Ilana diagnoses this innocuous bathroom euphemism as rape culture, with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: A tight, well-constructed joke about performative wokeness taken to absurdist extremes. Ilana's application of 'rape culture' to the most benign possible phrase is funny because of her absolute conviction and because it follows her earlier genuine rape culture point. The juxtaposition — serious feminist commentary applied to a bathroom euphemism — is sharp. High character integration and economy.

jokes.text: The stranger's husband met her 'at a Foot Locker in Times Square. She was just hanging out. She wasn't buying anything, she was just chilling.'
jokes.setup: Someone asks where the man met his wife/partner (the woman who just left)
jokes.punchline: The specificity of 'just chilling' at a Foot Locker in Times Square — one of the least romantic/prestigious places in New York — delivered as if it's a perfectly normal meet-cute
jokes.explanation: The 'just hanging out' / 'wasn't buying anything, just chilling' detail is what makes this: it implies something faintly suspicious about a woman who loiters at a Times Square Foot Locker, but the man tells it with complete pride. Good economy — it's a fully formed character sketch in two sentences.

jokes.text: Male Stacey and Abbi struggle to remove her extremely tight pants due to the heat — she has to hold the bed while he pulls on three
jokes.setup: They've moved to the bedroom; Male Stacey attempts to remove Abbi's pants
jokes.punchline: The pants are so heat-swollen onto her body that it requires a coordinated two-person structural operation to remove them
jokes.explanation: A good physical comedy escalation of the heat/sweat theme — the pants becoming a structural challenge requiring a countdown and a braced position is a great absurdist payoff for the episode's running heat gag. The comedy also derives from the unsexy mechanics of what is supposed to be a seduction scene.

jokes.text: Male Stacey tries to remove Abbi's pants; they won't come off because of the heat-induced sweat — 'They're really on there.' Stacey: 'I'm gonna hold the bed, you just pull, okay?'
jokes.setup: Abbi's pants have been cemented to her body by sweat from the heat
jokes.punchline: Removing her pants requires a coordinated two-person effort involving him bracing against the bed frame — the swamp ass has made her pants structurally part of her body
jokes.explanation: This pays off the swamp ass runner in a perfectly escalated way — first the hair dryer, now pants that require structural assistance to remove. The callback to 'sort of part of my body' (joke 15) is clean. Physical comedy that escalates naturally.

jokes.text: The paper towels reveal: Abbi confesses she shoved paper towels in her butt crack to absorb swamp ass and forgot they were there
jokes.setup: Male Stacey asks if she 'had an accident' when the paper towels are revealed
jokes.punchline: Abbi explains the full sequence of events — shoving paper towels into her butt crack as an improvised absorber, forgetting them, then having them discovered mid-undressing by a romantic partner
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's cringe centerpiece and earns 8+. It pays off a multi-scene setup (swamp ass established at 02:55, bathroom visit at 04:35 where she specifically mentions wiping, now the reveal). The confession is structured perfectly: Male Stacey's question ('Did you have an accident?'), then Abbi's explanation delivered in a single breathless run-on sentence that gets worse with every clause. 'So let's just pause. Let's just pause.' is the perfect Male Stacey response — not horrified, just... requesting a processing beat. The joke also has its own callback (the bathroom scene) that retroactively recontextualizes her panicked sentence-stopping. High impact because the audience has been half-expecting something like this since the swamp ass setup.

jokes.text: Male Stacey: 'That's a lot of paper towels. Do you recycle?'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just revealed the paper towel situation
jokes.punchline: Male Stacey's response is not shock or disgust but a practical environmental concern about paper waste
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and justifies 8+. In six words, Male Stacey: (1) confirms there are a LOT of paper towels (implying a scale beyond what we imagined), (2) pivots from the disgusting to the mundane with perfect deadpan, and (3) makes his environmental consciousness his primary reaction to what just happened. The economy is exceptional — 'Do you recycle?' is functionally the same length as any punchline could be, but the specific choice of recycling as the concern is inspired. It tells you everything about Male Stacey as a character. Comparable in economy and subversion to the 8.0 anchor example.

jokes.text: Ilana at the party confessional: 'I make more eye contact with this face than any human being's face. It's crazy. I love "watching" it.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is apparently interacting with something — context suggests she's with someone at the party or looking at something on a phone/screen
jokes.punchline: She makes 'watching' a verb with implied scare quotes, and the nature of what she's watching is left ambiguous — possibly something inappropriate
jokes.explanation: Context-dependent joke that doesn't fully land in transcript form — the visual component is missing. The 'watching' in quotes implies something worth noting but the transcript doesn't give us enough to score it highly.

jokes.text: Abbi calling out 'Male Stacey!' with increasing urgency during sex, building from 'Stacey' to 'Stacey!' to 'Stace?' to 'Male Stacey!'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Male Stacey are finally having sex
jokes.punchline: The escalating alarm in her voice, culminating in the full formal name 'Male Stacey!' — the qualifier that distinguishes him from female Stacey — signals something has gone very wrong
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'Stacey' to 'Male Stacey' is a great structural joke — the full qualifier only comes out when something is seriously wrong, which inverts normal naming convention (you'd expect the nickname at peak intimacy, not the formal signifier). It's also a show-specific running joke that Male Stacey is distinguished from Stacey by gender prefix, so using it at this moment is a perfect character-world joke.

jokes.text: The jackhammer punning scene: Ilana shouts 'So, to clarify, you raped—' and the jackhammer starts; when it stops, she says '—him.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is confessing to Ilana what happened with Male Stacey (he passed out from the heat mid-sex)
jokes.punchline: The word 'raped' gets drowned out by a jackhammer, with Ilana's voice returning on 'him' after the construction noise stops — a classic comedic sound censor substitute
jokes.explanation: A solid visual/sound comedy construction gag — the city itself censors the accusation, which is a good NYC-specific joke. The 'him' landing cleanly after the noise stops has good structural timing. Not highly original (it's a well-worn comedy device) but well-executed in context.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'That is literally what "they" say.' / Abbi: 'Yeah, but I really mean it.' / Ilana: 'So do "they".'
jokes.setup: Abbi insists Male Stacey 'seriously wanted it'; Ilana points out this is exactly what rapists say
jokes.punchline: Abbi's 'I really mean it' / Ilana's 'So do they' — the exchange is a two-beat knockout where Ilana's response neutralizes Abbi's defense with perfect economy
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through exceptional structure and economy. The exchange is a compressed logical trap: every defense Abbi offers is immediately identified as a stock rape apologetics phrase. 'So do they' is a perfect four-word demolition of Abbi's argument. The joke handles an extremely dark subject (actual non-consent) with enough comedic distance (the absurdity of the heat-wave accident context) to work as dark comedy. The 'they' in quotes is doing heavy lifting — it signals Ilana knows exactly what category she's in. This is the kind of exchange that makes the show distinctive.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'My God, I raped him, dude. I... I raped male Stacey. I'm a monster. I need an air conditioner, immediately!'
jokes.setup: Abbi has just processed that she is, by her own analysis, a rapist
jokes.punchline: The pivot from existential moral crisis ('I'm a monster') to immediate practical demand ('I need an air conditioner, immediately!') — the A/C need is what she lands on as the priority
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ as a structural joke that simultaneously pays off two threads: the moral crisis thread (Abbi confronting what she did) and the episode's central MacGuffin (the air conditioner). The logic is: if she'd had A/C, none of this would have happened; therefore the A/C is the most urgent need. The immediate pivot makes it funnier than a longer meltdown would. 'Wow, wow, wow, wow, wow!' from Ilana as a reaction is the perfect beat.

jokes.text: Ilana on moving the fans: 'I was gonna say something, but I didn't want to step on anyone's toes, you know, 'cause like, I don't work here.'
jokes.setup: They note that someone wisely moved the fans at a store to a better location
jokes.punchline: Ilana had the right idea but held back out of professional respect — despite having no professional connection to this establishment
jokes.explanation: A low-key character joke — Ilana's internal professional code ('I don't work here') applied to a retail store she's shopping in. The incongruity of taking 'step on anyone's toes' as a real professional concern in a place you're just browsing is good Ilana-specific logic.

jokes.text: Ilana considers a turbie twist for Lincoln, then: 'Probably not, 'cause he doesn't have any hair.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is shopping for Lincoln's birthday present and considers a turbie twist
jokes.punchline: The casual reveal that Lincoln is bald, delivered as an obvious afterthought to a genuinely-considered gift option
jokes.explanation: A light character-comedy beat. The implication that she seriously considered a turbie twist before remembering he's bald suggests a specific kind of charming obliviousness. Clean and economical.

jokes.text: Ilana's confession: 'We don't... we don't talk. We hook up, I smoke him up, we watch TV. It's great.'
jokes.setup: Abbi asks if Ilana talks to Lincoln, given that she didn't know about his blog
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'relationship' with Lincoln is apparently entirely non-verbal activities — and she describes this arrangement with complete satisfaction
jokes.explanation: 'It's great' as the final assessment of a relationship defined entirely by absence of communication is perfectly delivered. The enumeration ('hook up, smoke him up, watch TV') as a sufficient life partnership is good Ilana-world-building — she's genuinely satisfied by this arrangement, which makes it funnier than if she were in denial.

jokes.text: Abbi is greeted by name by multiple store employees with enthusiasm — it emerges she is a regular to an embarrassing degree; she denies coming in 'that much'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana enter what appears to be a home goods store to look for A/C
jokes.punchline: Multiple employees shout 'Abbi!' and 'Holler at me, Abbi!' revealing she is clearly a semi-resident of this store; she insists 'Not that much' with zero conviction
jokes.explanation: A good character-establishing scene — Abbi is apparently a fixture at this store, which she's mildly embarrassed by. The multiple employee greetings escalate into a whole social event that contradicts her 'not that much.' Strong character integration for Abbi.

jokes.text: An employee has named a cutting board 'the Abbi' — 'I've been calling it "the Abbi."'
jokes.setup: Abbi's embarrassing level of store regularity is being revealed through employee greetings
jokes.punchline: The store employee has named a product after her — the ultimate sign of brand loyalty crossed into something else
jokes.explanation: A strong escalation of the store-regularity gag — moving from 'employees know her name' to 'they named merchandise after her' is a well-structured comedic escalation. 'The Abbi' as a cutting board name also suggests she's either a particularly cutting person or particularly flat, neither of which is a flattering implication.

jokes.text: Ilana and an employee do a choreographed handshake/dance routine that Abbi doesn't know — 'Oh, my God, she doesn't do dances with me.'
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi are greeted by various employees; Ilana is clearly also a regular
jokes.punchline: Ilana has a personalized choreographed greeting with a store employee that Abbi isn't part of — Ilana and the employee have built something Abbi is excluded from
jokes.explanation: A visual gag with strong character comedy: Ilana has apparently cultivated a deeper relationship with this store employee than Abbi has, despite Abbi being the one with a product named after her. The betrayal read from Abbi's 'she doesn't do dances with me' is perfect.

jokes.text: Ilana's Facebook status draft: 'Seeking air conditioner, free to cheap. Thanks. Bye, guys.' — Abbi: '"Bye, guys" is my favorite part.'
jokes.setup: They're looking for a free or cheap A/C; Ilana proposes posting on Facebook
jokes.punchline: 'Bye, guys' as the sign-off to a desperate Facebook plea is an extremely specific piece of Ilana-voice — the casual farewell at the end of what is essentially an SOS
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ purely on character specificity and economy. 'Bye, guys' as the conclusion of a social media plea is a pinpoint-accurate portrait of a certain kind of early-2010s Facebook post. Abbi's observation ('that's my favorite part') as the immediate reaction is perfect — she sees the genius in it. The joke requires no explanation and says everything about how Ilana moves through the world: breezy, semi-oblivious to urgency, weirdly charming. Extremely quotable and reusable as a cultural reference.

jokes.text: Ilana and Abbi split up to find a cab: 'I'll look for a cab on the side streets, you take the avenue.' / 'Genius. All angles.' Ilana: 'Whoo!'
jokes.setup: They need to get the A/C home
jokes.punchline: 'Genius. All angles.' — they're treating 'check two streets' as a masterstroke of tactical planning
jokes.explanation: A functional joke about their tendency to treat mundane logistics as grand strategy. Mild humor, low stakes, competent execution.

jokes.text: Ilana screams 'Come on, New York! Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes! Brb! Ab, I got one! Right back here! Are you (bleep) kidding me?!' — celebrating finding a cab with full theatrical intensity
jokes.setup: They split up to find a cab
jokes.punchline: Ilana's cab-hailing is performed as if she's achieving something monumental — screaming at the city, broadcasting the triumph
jokes.explanation: Ilana's physical energy makes this work more than the writing. Her full-volume celebration of a cab as a victory is consistent character work. Works better as performance than as a written joke.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I want more panache. I want more spunk, more jizzy-jazz.' / Abbi: 'Eww, okay.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is critiquing Abbi's Facebook post draft for lacking personality
jokes.punchline: 'Jizzy-jazz' as a synonym for 'pizzazz' — a portmanteau that imports sexual content into a music/style metaphor
jokes.explanation: Coined slang is a Broad City specialty. 'Jizzy-jazz' is a good one — it sounds like 'pizzazz' but sexualizes it, and Abbi's 'Eww, okay' is the perfect response: not outraged, just mildly grossed out before accepting the term. High quotability as an exportable Ilana-ism.

jokes.text: Two people immediately respond to Ilana's Facebook post — Ilana: 'Oh, my God. That's so embarrassing. They are like up Facebook's butthole, no shame!'
jokes.setup: Ilana posted asking for an A/C on Facebook and gets instant responses
jokes.punchline: Ilana judges people for being on Facebook too much — having just posted on Facebook
jokes.explanation: A good hypocrisy joke — Ilana has zero self-awareness about being the person who just posted on Facebook. 'Up Facebook's butthole' is vivid enough to be quotable. The joke works because Ilana's contempt for the responders is completely unearned and she knows it without knowing it.

jokes.text: First response is from Ilana's old babysitter 'who, if I'm being honest, potentially broke up my parents.'
jokes.setup: Ilana checks who responded to her Facebook post
jokes.punchline: The casual, almost parenthetical accusation against her childhood babysitter of breaking up her family — treated as a minor logistical inconvenience in the context of A/C-hunting
jokes.explanation: The 'if I'm being honest, potentially' hedge before a serious family trauma accusation is the joke — maximum understatement applied to maximum stakes. The fact that this registers as a minor 'so, no' rather than a revelation tells us everything about Ilana's relationship to her own backstory. Strong economy: the whole sentence is the setup, punchline, and character essay simultaneously.

jokes.text: Ilana on the phone with Bevers to identify Benny Calitri: 'He was my "plus one"!' — and Bevers's reveal that he's 'one of my best friends'
jokes.setup: Ilana needs to identify a mutual Facebook friend named Benny Calitri
jokes.punchline: The mutual friend is the 'plus one' Bevers wouldn't let Ilana bring to his party — and he turns out to be one of Bevers's best friends, which means Bevers specifically blocked Ilana's best friend from his party
jokes.explanation: A good character-world joke that expands the Bevers universe. The implication that Bevers excluded Ilana's friend specifically while later calling him one of his 'best friends' reveals the passive-aggressive politics of their living arrangement. Solid setup/reveal structure.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'You know, I've never been to this neighborhood before, but I'm not scared, 'cause I'm with a stone cold rapist!'
jokes.setup: They're walking to pick up the A/C from Benny; Ilana is noting they're in an unfamiliar neighborhood
jokes.punchline: She reframes Abbi's accidental sexual misconduct as a street-safety asset — being with a 'stone cold rapist' is presented as protective in an unfamiliar neighborhood
jokes.explanation: A strong callback that inverts the expected emotional register. Ilana should be horrified; instead she's using Abbi's accidental crime as a personal security feature. This perfectly characterizes Ilana's loyalty — she processes Abbi's worst moment as an asset. The 'stone cold' modifier is excellent, implying habitual and professional-grade criminality. Earns 8+ because it turns a dark subplot into a loving character moment while also being genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'It's reverse rapism. You are raping rape culture.'
jokes.setup: Abbi protests 'okay, all right' to Ilana's rapist label
jokes.punchline: 'Reverse rapism' and 'raping rape culture' — Ilana's ideological framework retrofits Abbi's accidental assault into a feminist act
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ as a callback that brings together two of the episode's running threads — Ilana's rape culture commentary from the party scene and Abbi's accidental assault. 'Raping rape culture' is a genuinely novel construction that manages to be both an absurdist non-sequitur and internally consistent within Ilana's ideology. The coinage 'reverse rapism' (parallel to reverse racism) is the kind of linguistic invention that defines the show's voice. Only Ilana could deliver this with complete sincerity as a comfort to her friend.

jokes.text: Benny Calitri is filming a testimonial for his Amazing Race submission while giving away his A/C — 'I'm just a guy looking for a challenge that'll change his life forever.'
jokes.setup: They've arrived to pick up the A/C; the provider turns out to be a stranger doing reality TV prep
jokes.punchline: Benny is using a mundane Craigslist A/C handoff as footage for a reality show application — narrating it as if it's already compelling television
jokes.explanation: A solid one-episode character whose entire premise is comedic — treating everyday life as Amazing Race content. The joke is the gap between the mundanity of the situation and his self-narration of it as heroic. Functions as good supporting comedy for the A/C acquisition plot.

jokes.text: Benny recruits them to carry boxes to his truck in exchange for the A/C, then narrates their arrival to his camera: 'A couple of my best friends showed up. Even though they were pretty stacked, I've got to keep my eye on the prize...'
jokes.setup: Benny is getting free labor from Ilana and Abbi in exchange for the A/C
jokes.punchline: He describes them as 'my best friends' to his camera (they met 90 seconds ago) and credits himself for resisting their attractiveness in service of his 'Amazing Race' goals
jokes.explanation: The 'best friends' instant escalation of strangers is funny in its specificity. The 'even though they were pretty stacked' acknowledgment that he finds them attractive, followed by Amazing Race discipline, is a good character beat.

jokes.text: The A/C turns out to be broken — Benny assumed 'broke-ass' in the post meant 'broken'
jokes.setup: They've carried boxes, secured the A/C, and are ready to leave
jokes.punchline: 'Why do you guys want a broken air conditioner?' — Benny is 'not big into slang' and read 'broke-ass' as 'broken'
jokes.explanation: A well-planted callback payoff — the 'broke-ass' in the Facebook post was established early enough to be forgotten, making the reveal land as genuine surprise. The logic is tight: Benny 'not big into slang' reading 'broke-ass' literally is plausible, and the visual of them having carried boxes for nothing is a good physical comedy payoff. Solid structure.

jokes.text: Abbi to Benny about his mom dropping out of Amazing Race: 'Maybe your dad should have pulled out.'
jokes.setup: Benny mentions his mom 'just pulled out' of being his Amazing Race partner
jokes.punchline: Abbi delivers a birth control/contraception insult implying she wishes Benny hadn't been born — framed as casual wordplay on 'pulled out'
jokes.explanation: A classic wordplay trap — 'pulled out' has an obvious double meaning that Abbi deploys immediately. The meta-beat ('I just thought of that') shows she's delighted with herself. Ilana's 'Whoa, dude. That was awesome.' validates the audience reaction. 'I don't get it' from Benny is the perfect topper — it calls attention to the joke's vulgarity without requiring explanation. Good economy: six words, devastating.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'Maybe your dad should have pulled out.' Abbi: 'Whoa, dude.' Ilana: 'That was awesome. I just thought of that.'
jokes.setup: Benny says his mom 'pulled out' of Amazing Race
jokes.punchline: Ilana makes the 'your dad should have pulled out' birth-control joke, then celebrates her own quickness — the self-congratulation is as funny as the joke itself
jokes.explanation: The 'pulled out' joke itself is a fairly standard crude-pun response; what elevates it is Ilana's immediate self-congratulation ('that was awesome, I just thought of that') making her excitement the main joke. Benny's 'I don't get it' button is a nice topper — he's immune to her humor. Below-average originality for the core joke but the character frame saves it.

jokes.text: Ilana's spontaneous adoption theory: the A/C she left in college is like a baby given up for adoption — 'she should be able to go and get that baby back whenever she pleases.' Abbi: 'I can't... I can't get into adoption with you again.'
jokes.setup: Ilana reveals she left an A/C in her old college dorm and wants to retrieve it
jokes.punchline: Ilana has apparently argued adoption law before using consumer goods as the analogy; Abbi's 'I can't get into adoption with you again' implies this is a recurring debate
jokes.explanation: The joke is 'again' — Ilana apparently has strong opinions on adoption that she has already made Abbi sit through at least once. The A/C-as-relinquished-child metaphor is committed to and internally consistent, which makes it funnier. 'I can't... I can't get into adoption with you again' is an Abbi line that implies a whole prior conversation we didn't see, which is one of the show's best structural tricks.

jokes.text: Ilana tries to get Abbi into the college dorm by claiming they're 'loving lesbians' when the security guard questions the expired ID — then when he says he doesn't care about that, she pivots to 'I guess it's because we're straight, then? The straight girls of 2015 shafted yet again!'
jokes.setup: Security guard says Abbi's ID is expired
jokes.punchline: Ilana's discrimination framework escalates from 'you're homophobic' to 'you're straight-phobic' within seconds of being told neither applies — she needs to locate the oppression somewhere
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and justifies 8+. The structure is a perfect three-beat: (1) Ilana plays the discrimination card with conviction, (2) security guard neutralizes it ('I don't care if you're lesbians'), (3) Ilana immediately inverts to complaining about straight discrimination. 'The straight girls of 2015 shafted yet again!' is an inspired piece of culture-war absurdism — it mimics real identity politics language and deploys it in the exact wrong direction. High character integration: only Ilana would generate this specific sequence of defenses. 'Hetero-normative notion' as she's in the middle of calling them lesbians is also a great self-undermining detail. Extremely quotable and culturally specific to 2014/2015 discourse.

jokes.text: Security guard: 'You kids are all straight, and you're all gay.' Ilana: 'Thank you. Thank you, sir.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been arguing identity politics at the security desk for 30 seconds
jokes.punchline: The guard's zen-like 'you're all straight, you're all gay' cuts through Ilana's framework with bored omniscience, and she accepts it as a compliment
jokes.explanation: The security guard's line is a genuinely wise/funny rejoinder — it dismisses the entire category of identity politics debate with a tautology that's somehow satisfying. Ilana's 'Thank you. Thank you, sir' treating it as a profound personal affirmation is the perfect response — she hears it as validation rather than dismissal. Great economy: seven words that end an argument.

jokes.text: Ilana and Abbi pose as R.A.s in a dorm room 'raid' — Ilana: 'This is a spontaneous room raid. Whoo!'
jokes.setup: They need to get an A/C from a dorm room; they decide to bluff their way in as authority figures
jokes.punchline: Ilana announces the 'raid' with the enthusiasm of a party, undercutting any intimidation they might have had
jokes.explanation: The 'Whoo!' appended to an authority declaration is a perfect Ilana touch — she can't maintain even the pretense of seriousness. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Dorm room guy claims his uncle is Adam Corolla as apparent intimidation — delivered as a credible threat
jokes.setup: Ilana and Abbi have announced themselves as R.A.s doing a raid; a dorm resident tries to intimidate them
jokes.punchline: His counter is to name-drop Adam Carolla — a moderately famous podcaster — as if this is a genuine deterrent to authority figures
jokes.explanation: The joke is the absurd specificity of Adam Carolla as the celebrity connection deployed in a crisis. Not a movie star, not a politician — a podcast host. The confidence of the delivery ('pretty much' when asked if he's a comedian) sets up the Carolla drop well. Ilana's whispered 'That's it' acknowledging it as the only retort needed is funny.

jokes.text: The fake room raid escalates: Abbi finds vodka — 'Mad vodka, huh? Good stuff, huh? And it's also against the rules. Strike uno!'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are performing a fake room raid
jokes.punchline: 'Strike uno' — Abbi uses Spanish for 'one' mid-authoritative speech, a small verbal break in the performance that makes the fake authority funnier
jokes.explanation: 'Strike uno' is a small, funny inconsistency in Abbi's fake authority mode. The 'Janet's 40' birthday reference (unclear context) mixed in adds to the chaotic improvisational feel. Mid-level joke.

jokes.text: Ilana sniffs out weed hidden in the wall, Lassie-style — 'What is it, girl? What is it?'
jokes.setup: Ilana appears to be sniffing around the dorm room
jokes.punchline: Abbi addresses her like a dog following a scent, and it turns out Ilana has sniffed out weed hidden in the wall — her marijuana-detection sense is apparently dog-like
jokes.explanation: A strong character-integrated visual gag — the Lassie comparison is perfect because it implies Ilana has a special relationship with marijuana that transcends normal sensory capabilities. The wall weed discovery is a great payoff: 'Who would leave weed in a wall? A weed genius!' reveals Ilana planted it herself at some point. This retroactively makes her interest in the dorm even funnier.

jokes.text: 'Who would leave weed in a wall, anyway?' — Ilana: 'A weed genius! And she'd leave it there indefinitely, in case of emergency.'
jokes.setup: The dorm residents disclaim the wall weed
jokes.punchline: Ilana defends the practice in such specific detail ('she'd leave it indefinitely, in case of emergency') that it's immediately clear she is the weed genius in question
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ for misdirection structure. The joke uses the dorm residents' denial to set up Ilana's passionate defense of the unnamed 'weed genius' — the specificity of 'she'd leave it there indefinitely' is a tell that creates the reveal without stating it. The audience figures it out half a beat before she'd confirm it. 'Weed genius' is a good coinage. Strong character integration: of course Ilana pre-positioned emergency marijuana in a college dorm wall.

jokes.text: Abbi and Ilana decide not to report the violations, then 'punish' the students by making them smoke all the weed in a bong hotbox
jokes.setup: As fake R.A.s, they have 'found' weed and alcohol; Ilana suggests they 'revoke A/C privileges' as punishment
jokes.punchline: The 'punishment' is Ilana forcing the students to smoke her recovered wall weed in a bong — presented with complete authoritative seriousness ('I'm gonna teach you boys about the dangers')
jokes.explanation: The 'punishment' inversion — using authority to force students to do something enjoyable (smoke weed) while presenting it as discipline — is a good escalation of the R.A. premise. 'Ripping underaged bongs' as official R.A. terminology is funny specific language. The whole scheme pays off: they get the A/C, recover the wall weed, and give the students a party. It's a complete comedic arc.

jokes.text: Resident: 'Also... what does this have to do with the A/C?' Abbi: 'These are the rules, and when you graduate and get a real job like an R.A., we can talk about it.' Resident: 'R.A.s are students.' Abbi: 'When you graduate, we can talk about what an R.A. is and isn't.'
jokes.setup: The residents are questioning the fake authority
jokes.punchline: The resident correctly points out R.A.s are students; Abbi doubles down on her wrong version of R.A.s as an authority to be explained 'when he graduates,' with no logic
jokes.explanation: A good cringe moment as Abbi's fake authority is challenged on a factual basis and she digs in rather than adjusting. Her commitment to the wrong position is character-true and funny.

jokes.text: As punishment, Ilana forces the dorm residents to smoke 'the whole bag' — she's basically hotboxing them with her own weed, recovered from the wall
jokes.setup: The 'punishment' for the violations is Ilana making them smoke the confiscated weed
jokes.punchline: The 'disciplinary action' is forcing underage students to smoke weed — Ilana treats this as a stern consequence while it's also clearly something she'd do for fun
jokes.explanation: The inversion of 'punishment' (forced weed smoking) is the joke's core. Ilana's poker-faced delivery of a bong as punishment while clearly enjoying the authority is strong character comedy. The seriousness of 'you're gonna smoke the whole bag' applied to something pleasurable is the comedic tension.

jokes.text: Ilana on her app idea: 'I invented this app... Well, okay, I didn't invent it yet, but soon. It's this photo app that adds lasers to images — lasers coming out of eyes, or nipples, or buttholes.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is pitching an app idea at what seems to be a dinner/date setting
jokes.punchline: The correction ('I didn't invent it yet, but soon') is the first joke; the app itself (laser nipples and butthole imagery) is the second; the escalating specificity of body parts is the third
jokes.explanation: The invention claim retraction ('I didn't invent it yet') is the perfect confidence-then-correction beat. The app itself — laser nipples/buttholes — is Ilana at her most entrepreneurial and disgusting. The escalation from eyes (normal laser destination) to nipples to buttholes as the list-of-three is textbook comedic structure with Ilana-specific content. Her companion's 'That'd be pretty cool, if it wasn't already an app' is the perfect deadpan topper.

jokes.text: Her companion: 'That'd be pretty cool, if it wasn't already an app.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has pitched her laser app idea with enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: It already exists — the idea isn't original, deflating Ilana's entrepreneurial pitch
jokes.explanation: Clean, economic punchline for the app bit. 'If it wasn't already an app' is delivered as a non-judgmental informational statement, which makes it a softer but more deflating landing than mockery would be.

jokes.text: Ilana claims to have invented Bleepy; companion says 'Of course! Bleepy is huge!' Then Ilana: 'It's obviously not my only capitalist venture at this time.'
jokes.setup: After the failed app pitch, Ilana pivots to claiming she invented another app
jokes.punchline: 'It's obviously not my only capitalist venture at this time' — corporate-speak delivered by someone who has just been caught lying about inventing things
jokes.explanation: The companion's credulous 'Of course! Bleepy is huge!' followed by Ilana's smooth transition to corporate-speak ('not my only capitalist venture') is a good con-artist-in-miniature beat. The 'at this time' is the perfect corporate verbal tic applied to Ilana's fake business empire. 'Eww' from the companion as a response to 'capitalist venture' is funny — they find the phrase distasteful.

jokes.text: Ilana asks the companion's age; he says 16. Ilana: 'Oh, I've done it again.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been flirting with someone she met at the dorm party; she asks how old he is
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, I've done it again' — delivered with the tired resignation of someone for whom this is a recurring pattern
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and genuinely earns 8+. The entire joke is five words: 'Oh, I've done it again.' The joke depends entirely on character — Ilana's reaction to accidentally hitting on a minor is not horror or embarrassment, but the weary recognition of a familiar mistake ('again'). This implies a history. The delivery performs total self-knowledge without any self-improvement. It's the comedy of someone who has been here before and knows it. Economy is near-perfect: there is no shorter version of this joke that would work better. Highly quotable and completely character-specific.

jokes.text: Ilana: 'I'm a repeated sex offender. I'm a monster, and I just have to deal with that, so...'
jokes.setup: Ilana has processed that she's flirted with a 16-year-old
jokes.punchline: The shrug-and-move-on structure of 'I'm a monster, and I just have to deal with that, so...' — the 'so' trails off into acceptance rather than resolution
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ as a character piece and a callback. The 'so...' trailing off into nothing is the joke — she's processing by narrating, but there's no actual plan. Also functions as a mirror to Abbi's 'I'm a monster' from id:30, creating an accidental thematic echo: both women have accidentally become sex offenders today. 'Repeated sex offender' implies the 16-year-old is not the first. The matter-of-fact acceptance is quintessential Ilana.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'So, you made out with a minor. You finished a couple of moments after a dude went unconscious. You're a sex offender at worst! Welcome to the club.' Ilana: 'There's a club?'
jokes.setup: Both women are processing their respective sexual misconduct situations
jokes.punchline: Abbi's 'at worst' minimization followed by the 'welcome to the club' — and Ilana's delighted 'There's a club?'
jokes.explanation: A strong convergence joke that pays off the entire episode's sex offender running gag. 'At worst' as the qualifier before 'sex offender' is doing dark comedy work — it's a minimization that doesn't minimize. 'Welcome to the club' sets up Ilana's excited 'There's a club?' — she hears opportunity where Abbi meant solidarity. Then 'it's not hard to get into' is the topper that earns both laughs. The exchange is also a moment of genuine friendship: Abbi is comforting Ilana in the language of shared transgression.

jokes.text: The meal card stolen from 'Reginald Corolla' has $3,600 on it — Ilana: 'This asshole's got $3,600 on his meal card.'
jokes.setup: They've apparently stolen a university meal card
jokes.punchline: The surname 'Corolla' connects back to the 'Adam Corolla' uncle claim from id:54 — and the $3,600 balance is the punchline, implying shocking parental wealth/neglect
jokes.explanation: A good callback — 'Reginald Corolla' connects the meal card theft to the Adam Corolla name-drop from the dorm room raid. The $3,600 figure is funny as specific evidence of extreme privilege. 'This asshole' is Ilana's instinctive class-consciousness.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'I am honored and thrilled that you would steal something for me.' Ilana: 'I feel like a fugitive.' Abbi: '...And if anyone asks, I'll say the one-armed man took it.'
jokes.setup: Abbi accepts the gift of a stolen meal card
jokes.punchline: 'The one-armed man took it' — a reference to The Fugitive, which Ilana doesn't get at all
jokes.explanation: Ilana's 'I feel like a fugitive' sets up Abbi's Fugitive callback naturally. The joke within the joke is that Ilana doesn't get the reference ('You don't get it, do you?' / 'No, I don't get it at all') — they're both laughing but for different reasons, which is a perfect micro-portrait of their dynamic.

jokes.text: Abbi introduces the cats to their new bed (her sweatshirt): 'You got me, daddy-maddy. You got Odysseus, Medusa, Artemis, Hermes. You got Drew—' (horrified pause) 'Guys, where is Drew?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is home, introducing her cats to a new sleeping arrangement using her sweatshirt
jokes.punchline: The roll call of mythologically named cats is interrupted by the panicked realization that 'Drew' — the one non-mythological cat — is missing. Drew is the joke by contrast.
jokes.explanation: Earns 8+ through a beautifully constructed list joke. The setup is Abbi rattling off impressively mythological cat names (Odysseus, Medusa, Artemis, Hermes), building a comedic rhythm and character portrait (she is educated, pretentious about naming). Then the fifth cat is 'Drew.' The contrast between four Greek/Roman deities and 'Drew' is itself the joke — Drew is either adopted or she ran out of ideas. Then the missing Drew panic ('Guys, where is Drew?') is the perfect second punchline that reveals she cares equally about all of them. The specificity of 'daddy-maddy' as her self-title is also funny. This is well-crafted structural comedy.

jokes.text: The air conditioner is installed and it's so cold it's 'like a whole other apartment' — they stand in front of it in ecstasy
jokes.setup: After the entire episode's quest for A/C, it's finally installed
jokes.punchline: The payoff is rapturous — 'So good. So good. So good!' — the A/C is treated as a transformative life experience after the heat ordeal
jokes.explanation: A satisfying emotional payoff for the episode's MacGuffin. The comedy is in the intensity of the reaction — coughing from cold, 'like a whole other apartment,' 'So good!' repeated three times — treating A/C as a transcendent experience. Good closure on the heat theme.

jokes.text: Male Stacey is revealed to have been in Abbi's apartment the whole time, watching them ecstatically enjoy the A/C — 'Who the (bleep) are you?' / 'How did you get into my apartment?' / 'You (bleep) perv! Get in that box!'
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are having a cathartic A/C moment when they notice they're not alone
jokes.punchline: Male Stacey has apparently been in the apartment watching them the entire time — the man Abbi 'raped' is now hiding in her apartment as she celebrates her accidental crime
jokes.explanation: A strong closing joke that brings the episode's two main threads together — Male Stacey and the A/C — in an unexpected collision. The horror of realizing you've been observed turns the joyful A/C scene retroactively uncomfortable. 'Get in that box!' as Abbi's first instinct is a great line — redirecting the discovery into an order. 'Are you sure that's even a cat?' as the final line is a perfect button, questioning whether they can trust their own perceptions after the whole day. Strong structure as an episode closer.
